hallucinations.description: The paper cites 'CodeScaler (Zhu et al., 2026)' with a future publication date of 2026 and an arXiv ID 'arXiv:2602.17684'. This paper cannot exist as of the time of writing, and the arXiv ID format '2602' would correspond to February 2026, which is in the future. This appears to be a fabricated reference.
hallucinations.evidence: Zhu, X., Zhou, X., Zhu, B., Hu, H., Du, M., Zhang, H., Wang, H., and Guo, Z. (2026). CodeScaler: Scaling code LLM training and test-time inference via execution-free reward models. *arXiv preprint arXiv:2602.17684*.

hallucinations.description: The paper cites 'CodeRL+ (Jiang et al., 2025)' with arXiv ID 'arXiv:2510.18471'. While the date is plausible (October 2025), this specific paper and its claims (e.g., '4.6% average relative improvement in pass@1' and 'variable-level execution trajectories') cannot be verified. The arXiv ID '2510' corresponds to October 2025 which may not yet exist. This is likely a fabricated reference.
hallucinations.evidence: Jiang, X., Dong, Y., Liu, M., Deng, H., Wang, T., Tao, Y., Cao, R., Li, B., Jin, Z., Jiao, W., Huang, F., Li, Y., and Li, G. (2025). CodeRL+: Improving code generation via reinforcement with execution semantics alignment. *arXiv preprint arXiv:2510.18471*.

hallucinations.description: The paper cites 'Kulkarni and Srikumar, 2025' with arXiv ID 'arXiv:2506.06093' and specific claims about improving SQL accuracy from 31.49% to 49.83%. The arXiv ID '2506' corresponds to June 2025, which may not yet exist. This is likely a fabricated reference.
hallucinations.evidence: Kulkarni, A. and Srikumar, V. (2025). Reinforcing code generation: Improving text-to-SQL with execution-based learning. *arXiv preprint arXiv:2506.06093*.

hallucinations.description: The paper claims the reward combiner is 'a learned two-layer MLP combiner (g_\\psi) trained on human pairwise preference annotations using the Bradley-Terry model.' However, the actual implementation uses fixed weights (L1: 15%, L2: 20%, L3: 45%, L4: 20%) with no learning or human preference annotations involved. While the paper does acknowledge this discrepancy in Section 3.3, the abstract and introduction present the learned combiner as a core contribution without adequate qualification, creating a misleading impression of the actual methodology.
hallucinations.evidence: In experiments, we implement g_\\psi as a fixed weighted combination (L1: 15%, L2: 20%, L3: 45%, L4: 20%) as an approximation of the learned combiner.

hallucinations.description: The paper describes Level 4 (Semantic Distance) as using 'execution trace embeddings' with a 'contrastive InfoNCE objective' and cosine similarity between trace embeddings. However, the actual implementation uses a simple heuristic AST feature scorer that assigns fixed scores based on whether the code has a function definition (+0.3), return statement (+0.2), control flow (+0.15), etc. This is a crude heuristic, not an embedding-based similarity measure. While acknowledged in the text, the methodology section presents the trace embedding approach as the primary method.
hallucinations.evidence: In the experimental validation, we implement r_{\\text{sem}} using AST-based structural features as a computationally tractable proxy that captures syntactic structure, identifier naming, and control flow patterns.

hallucinations.description: The paper claims PPO fine-tuning was conducted as part of the HierAlign framework (Section 3.4 describes the PPO setup in detail), but the limitations section reveals that no actual RL training was performed. The experiments only evaluate reward signal quality without any PPO fine-tuning. The paper's framing as a 'post-training framework' is misleading since no training was actually done.
hallucinations.evidence: **No online RL training**: The experiments evaluate reward signal quality without actual PPO fine-tuning. The true benefit requires observing convergence speed and final policy quality improvements, which remain as future work.

hallucinations.description: The paper's analysis in Section 6.1 claims '10% of all evaluated solutions (12 out of 120) pass zero test cases.' Looking at the experimental results JSON, there are exactly 12 solutions with 0 passed tests, which is 10% of 120. However, the paper claims these are all from the 'partial solution' category and implies they are primarily low-quality solutions. Examining the data, some of these zero-pass solutions are actually from 'high' quality level (e.g., HE_7 high attempt 0 and 1, HE_13 high attempt 0 and 1), which undermines the paper's narrative about temperature-controlled quality levels being a reliable proxy.
hallucinations.evidence: The most critical finding is the contrast in partial solution rewards: 10% of all evaluated solutions (12 out of 120) pass zero test cases.

overall_assessment: The paper contains several hallucinations of varying severity. Most critically, it includes at least one clearly fabricated citation (CodeScaler, Zhu et al., 2026) with a future date, and two other citations that are likely fabricated (CodeRL+ and Kulkarni & Srikumar, both 2025 with specific arXiv IDs that may not exist). The methodology section describes sophisticated components (learned reward combiner with Bradley-Terry training, execution trace embeddings with InfoNCE, PPO fine-tuning) that were not actually implemented—the real implementation uses fixed weights, simple AST heuristics, and no RL training. While the paper does acknowledge some of these gaps, the framing in the abstract and introduction is misleading. The experimental results themselves appear to be genuine (matching the code and logs), but the paper overstates the sophistication of the implemented system.
